questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the TestAndSet() hardware instruction? Briefly explain how it is used to implement mutual exclusion.
keyPoints: The TestAndSet() instruction is an atomic hardware operation used to implement synchronization primitives like locks.
It works by reading the current value of a boolean variable (lock) and setting it to 'true' in a single, uninterruptible cycle.
A thread can acquire a lock only if TestAndSet() returns 'false', indicating the lock was free. If it returns 'true', the thread must wait (e.g., spin in a loop).

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Swap() and CompareAndSwap() hardware synchronization instructions. Explain the key operational difference between them and a scenario where one might be preferred.
keyPoints: Both Swap() and CompareAndSwap() (CAS) are atomic hardware instructions used for building synchronization mechanisms.
The Swap() instruction atomically exchanges the value of a register with a value in memory.
The CompareAndSwap() instruction is more complex; it only swaps the values if the current value in memory matches an expected 'old' value provided by the caller.

CAS is often preferred for implementing lock-free and wait-free algorithms because it can perform a conditional update, reducing the chance of busy-waiting and enabling more sophisticated non-blocking data structures.

questions.question: Explain how the Bounded Waiting condition for mutual exclusion can be violated in a simple spinlock implementation using TestAndSet(). Describe a specific hardware instruction (like FetchAndAdd()) and detail an algorithm that uses it to build a lock which guarantees bounded waiting.
keyPoints: A simple TestAndSet() lock uses a single shared lock variable. When the lock is released, any waiting thread could win the race to acquire it, potentially causing a thread to wait indefinitely (starvation), thus violating bounded waiting.
The FetchAndAdd() instruction atomically increments a variable and returns its previous value.
A ticket lock can be built using two shared variables: 'ticket' (serves number) and 'turn' (now serving number).
A thread acquires a ticket number using FetchAndAdd() on the 'ticket' variable. It then spins, waiting until its ticket number equals the 'turn' variable.

When a thread releases the lock, it increments 'turn'. This ensures threads are granted the lock in the order they requested it (FIFO), guaranteeing bounded waiting as each thread has a finite number of others ahead of it.
